column8: Unlike their Alolan counterparts, the Mareanie of the Galar region have not yet figured out that the branches of Corsola are delicious.
column9: It plunges the poison spike on its head into its prey. When the prey has weakened, Mareanie deals the finishing blow with its 10 tentacles.
column10: This Pokémon wanders the seaside looking for food. It often gets electric shocks from broken Pincurchin spines that it tries to eat.

column3: With its 12 legs, it creates a dome to shelter within. The flow of the tides doesn't affect Toxapex in there, so it's very comfortable.
column4: Toxapex crawls along the ocean floor on its 12 legs. It leaves a trail of Corsola bits scattered in its wake.
column5: To survive in the cold waters of Galar, this Pokémon forms a dome with its legs, enclosing its body so it can capture its own body heat.
column6: Toxapex gets into fierce battles with Bruxish over areas where warm ocean currents flow, but the odds are always against it.
column7: Within the poison sac in its body is a poison so toxic that Pokémon as large as Wailord will still be suffering three days after it first takes effect.
column8: This Pokémon lives within a dome made by its own legs. Toxapex monitors its surroundings by sensing the flow of the tide through its spikes.
column9: To attack, this Pokémon sends toxic spikes flying at its enemies. Ones that come close get mown down by the claws on its feet.
column10: Those attacked by Toxapex's poison will suffer intense pain for three days and three nights. Post-recovery, there will be some aftereffects.

column3: It has a stubborn, individualistic disposition. Eating dirt, making mud, and playing in the mire all form part of its daily routine.
column4: It loves playing in the mud. If it isn't showered with mud on a daily basis, it gets stressed out and stops listening to its Trainer.
column5: Loads weighing up to 50 times as much as its own body weight pose no issue for this Pokémon. It's skilled at making use of mud.

column7: Though a slow walker, Mudbray is plenty strong. Its pace doesn't change even when it's loaded with 50 times its own body weight.
column8: The mud stuck to Mudbray's hooves enhances its grip and its powerful running gait.
column9: Possessing mighty power, Mudbray can be loaded with 50 times its own body weight and will be totally fine.
column10: This Pokémon covers itself in mud that it has regurgitated. The mud won’t dry out even if it’s exposed to the sun for a long time.

column3: This Pokémon has been treasured not just for its physical labor but also because it produces high-quality mud used for making pottery.
column4: It remains calm and unmoving no matter the situation. It mixes dirt with the saliva in its mouth to make a special kind of mud.
column5: Mud that hardens around a Mudsdale's legs sets harder than stone. It's so hard that it allows this Pokémon to scrap a truck with a single kick.
column6: It spits a mud that provides resistance to both wind and rain, so the walls of old houses were often coated with it.
column7: Mudsdale has so much stamina that it could carry over 10 tons across the Galar region without rest or sleep.
column8: It can trudge mountain roads without rest for three days and three nights, all the while dragging a 10-ton load.
column9: Its legs are fortified with mud and harder than stone, and they can reduce a large truck to scrap with one kick.
column10: Its heavy, mud-covered kicks are its best means of attack, and it can reduce large trucks to scrap without breaking a sweat.

column3: Dewpider normally lives underwater. When it comes onto land in search of food, it takes water with it in the form of a bubble on its head.
column4: It supports the water bubble that envelops its head with three of its legs. Sometimes it secretly swaps which legs are holding up the bubble.
column5: Dewpider uses sticky threads to create the water bubble that covers its head, so the bubble very rarely bursts.
column6: When it comes across enemies or potential prey, this Pokémon smashes its water-bubble-covered head into them.
column7: It forms a water bubble at the rear of its body and then covers its head with it. Meeting another Dewpider means comparing water-bubble sizes.
column8: It crawls onto the land in search of food. Its water bubble allows it to breathe and protects its soft head.
column9: It can only breathe oxygen that has dissolved in water, so it wears a water bubble on its head when it walks around on land.
column10: When two Dewpider meet, they display their water bubbles to each other. Then the one with the smaller bubble gets out of the other's way.

column3: Despite what its appearance suggests, it cares for others. If it finds vulnerable, weak Pokémon, it protectively brings them into its water bubble.
column4: A caring Pokémon, Araquanid carries Dewpider around inside its water bubble to protect them as it walks. It protects its food in its bubble too.
column5: It launches water bubbles with its legs, drowning prey within the bubbles. This Pokémon can then take its time to savor its meal.
column6: It acts as a caretaker for Dewpider, putting them inside its bubble and letting them eat any leftover food.
column7: It launches sticky water bubbles. Foes who lose consciousness while trapped inside these bubbles will get torn apart by Araquanid’s gnashing jaws.
column8: It usually passes its time in the water. When its belly is full, it stores its subdued prey in the water bubble on its head.
column9: It delivers headbutts with the water bubble on its head. Small Pokémon get sucked into the bubble, where they drown.

column3: Fomantis hates having its naps interrupted. It fires off beams using energy it gathers by bathing in the sun.
column4: During the day, Fomantis basks in sunlight and sleeps peacefully. It wakes and moves around at night.
column5: Many Trainers give their Fomantis their own flowerpots so they can sunbathe in peace and quiet.
column6: They give off a sweet and refreshing scent. Cutiefly often gather near the tall grass where Fomantis are hiding.
column7: During the day, it sleeps and soaks up light. When night falls, it walks around looking for a safer place to sleep.
column8: When the sun rises, Fomantis spreads its four leaves and bathes in the sunlight. The tip of its head has a pleasant aroma.
column9: When bathed in sunlight, this Pokémon emits a pleasantly sweet scent, which causes bug Pokémon to gather around it.
column10: It loves the sunshine. When it basks in the sunlight regularly, it grows up with vivid coloration.

column3: It fires beams from its sickle-shaped petals. These beams are powerful enough to cleave through thick metal plates.
column4: By masquerading as a bug Pokémon, it lowers the guard of actual bug Pokémon lured in by a scent of sweet flowers. Its sickles bring them down.
column5: A lot of time and effort is required to maintain the vivid colors of its petals. This Pokémon puts its Trainer's attentiveness to the test.

column7: It requires a lot of effort to maintain Lurantis's vivid coloring, but some collectors enjoy this work and treat it as their hobby.
column8: This Pokémon resembles a beautiful flower. A properly raised Lurantis will have gorgeous, brilliant colors.
column9: As it slashes into its enemies, it looks like it's dancing. Its elegant appearance has led some to call it the most glamorous Grass Pokémon.
column10: The petals on this Pokémon's arms are thin and super sharp, and they can fire laser beams if Lurantis gathers light first.

column3: It likes damp dark places. When night falls, the spores filling the caps of its mushrooms glow.
column4: As it drowses the day away, it nourishes itself by sucking from tree roots. It wakens at the fall of night, wandering off in search of a new tree.
column5: Pokémon living in the forest eat the delicious caps on Morelull's head. The caps regrow overnight.
column6: Morelull live in forests that stay dark even during the day. They scatter flickering spores that put enemies to sleep.
column7: It scatters spores that flicker and glow. Anyone seeing these lights falls into a deep slumber.
column8: It scatters its shining spores around itself. Even though they're dangerous, nighttime tours of forests where Morelull live are popular.

column3: Forests where Shiinotic live are treacherous to enter at night. People confused by its strange lights can never find their way home again.
column4: It emits flickering spores that cause drowsiness. When its prey succumb to sleep, this Pokémon feeds on them by sucking in their energy.
column5: Its flickering spores lure in prey and put them to sleep. Once this Pokémon has its prey snoozing, it drains their vitality with its fingertips.
column6: It puts its prey to sleep and siphons off their vitality through the tip of its arms. If one of its kind is weakened, it helps by sending it vitality.

column3: This sneaky Pokémon will slink behind its prey and immobilize it with poisonous gas before the prey even realizes Salandit is there.
column4: Volcanoes or dry, craggy places are its home. It emanates a sweet-smelling poisonous gas that attracts bug Pokémon, then attacks them.
column5: Only female Salandit can produce gas laden with pheromones. Males entranced by this gas will do whatever the females tell them.
column6: The males will do whatever the females tell them. They give the females most of their food. Due to malnutrition, the males can't evolve.
column7: It weakens opponents and sends them reeling with its poisonous gas. Salandit doesn't deal well with Spinda, since it's always reeling.
column8: It taunts its prey and lures them into narrow, rocky areas where it then sprays them with toxic gas to make them dizzy and take them down.
column9: It burns its bodily fluids to create a poisonous gas. When its enemies become disoriented from inhaling the gas, it attacks them.
column10: Its venom sacs produce a fluid that this Pokémon then heats up with the flame in its tail. This process creates Salandit's poisonous gas.

column3: Only female Salazzle exist. They emit a gas laden with pheromones to captivate male Salandit.
column4: When two Salazzle meet, they will use their pheromone gas to fight over the males in each other's group.
column5: Filled with pheromones, its poisonous gas can be diluted to use in the production of luscious perfumes.
column6: Salazzle lives deep in caves and forces the Salandit it has attracted with its pheromones to serve it.
column7: The winner of competitions between Salazzle is decided by which one has the most male Salandit with it.
column8: Salazzle makes its opponents light-headed with poisonous gas, then captivates them with alluring movements to turn them into loyal servants.
column9: It punishes Salandit that couldn't bring it food with a fierce slap of its flame-spewing palm.
column10: For some reason, only females have been found. It creates a reverse harem of male Salandit that it lives with.

column3: It boasts power enough to split large trees in half. The organ on its rear releases an odor that it uses to communicate with others of its kind.
column4: A touch from anyone except a known friend sends it into a surging frenzy. It's an incredibly dangerous Pokémon.
column5: Stufful hates to be hugged or touched in any way. It will squirm, thrash, and resist with amazing power.
column6: Despite its adorable appearance, when it gets angry and flails about, its arms and legs could knock a pro wrestler sprawling.
column7: Its fluffy fur is a delight to pet, but carelessly reaching out to touch this Pokémon could result in painful retaliation.
column8: The way it protects itself by flailing its arms may be an adorable sight, but stay well away. This is flailing that can snap thick tree trunks.

column3: It boasts tremendous physical strength. Many people call it the most dangerous Pokémon in the Alola region.
column4: This immensely dangerous Pokémon possesses overwhelming physical strength. Its habitat is generally off-limits.
column5: The moves it uses to take down its prey would make a martial artist jealous. It tucks subdued prey under its arms to carry them to its nest.
column6: Once it accepts you as a friend, it tries to show its affection with a hug. Letting it do that is dangerous—it could easily shatter your bones.
column7: This Pokémon has the habit of hugging its companions. Many Trainers have left this world after their spines were squashed by its hug.
column8: It waves its hands wildly in intimidation and warning. Life is over for anyone who doesn't run away as fast as possible.

column3: A Greedent drawn in by the sweet scent of these Pokémon will tuck the Bounsweet in among the berries in its tail and carry them all away.
column4: Although it's too sugary for human consumption, Bounsweet's sweat can be watered down into a juice with just the right amount of sweetness.
column5: When under attack, it secretes a sweet and delicious sweat. The scent only calls more enemies to it.
column6: Its sweat is sweet, like syrup made from boiled-down fruit. Because of this, Bounsweet was highly valued in the past, when sweeteners were scarce.
column7: Because of its sweet, delicious aroma, bird Pokémon are always after it, but it's not intelligent enough to care.
column8: A delectable aroma pours from its body. They are often swallowed whole by Toucannon lured by that wafting deliciousness.
column9: Its body gives off a sweet, fruity scent that is extremely appetizing to bird Pokémon.
column10: It tries to resist being swallowed by a bird Pokémon by spinning the sepals on its head. It's usually a fruitless attempt.

column3: As it twirls like a dancer, a sweet smell spreads out around it. Anyone who inhales the scent will feel a surge of happiness.
column4: The sepals on its head developed to protect its body. These are quite hard, so even if pecked by bird Pokémon, this Pokémon is totally fine.
column5: This Pokémon is always bouncing around energetically. Other Pokémon are attracted by its lively appearance and pleasant aroma.
column6: It's protected by its hard sepals, so it plays with bird Pokémon without worry. They peck it relentlessly, but it doesn't care.
column7: Steenee bounces energetically through forests. If the rind that peels off its body is pulverized, it can be used to treat stomach pains.
column8: Any Corvisquire that pecks at this Pokémon will be greeted with a smack from its sepals followed by a sharp kick.
column9: Steenee spreads a sweet scent that makes others feel invigorated. This same scent is popular for antiperspirants.

column3: This Pokémon launches fierce yet elegant kicks with its long, slender legs. It views Quaquaval as its rival.
column4: Its long, striking legs aren't just for show but to be used to kick with skill. In victory, it shows off by kicking the defeated, laughing boisterously.
column5: A kick from the hardened tips of this Pokémon's legs leaves a wound in the opponent's body and soul that will never heal.
column6: This Pokémon is proud and aggressive. However, it is said that a Tsareena will instantly become calm if someone touches the crown on its calyx.
column7: A master of grand and beautiful kicks, it can knock out even kickboxing champions with a single blow.
column8: A Pokémon known for the beauty of its well-shaped legs, it sometimes appears as a mascot in advertisements for beauty salons.
column9: A Pokémon with an aggressive personality and a mastery of kicking, it cackles every time it kicks an opponent.
column10: This feared Pokémon has long, slender legs and a cruel heart. It shows no mercy as it stomps on its opponents.

column3: It stretches sticky vines out from its head and picks flowers to adorn itself with. When it doesn't have any flowers, it feels uneasy.
column4: Baths prepared with the flowers from its vine have a relaxing effect, so this Pokémon is a hit with many people.
column5: It prefers areas with lots of flowers. A Comfey adorned with flowers is a happy Comfey, and the flowers gain healing effects.
column6: Comfey picks flowers with its vine and decorates itself with them. For some reason, flowers won't wither once they're attached to a Comfey.
column7: These Pokémon smell very nice. All Comfey wear different flowers, so each of these Pokémon has its own individual scent.
column8: It uses its vine to pick flowers. The flowers that Comfey attaches to itself develop healing properties.
column9: It picks flowers and sticks them to its body with fluids it produces. The aroma that wafts from its petals has a healing effect.
column10: It attaches flowers to its highly nutritious vine. This revitalizes the flowers, and they give off an aromatic scent.

column3: People used to mistake Oranguru for a human when they saw it issue command after command to the other Pokémon of the forest.

column5: With waves of its fan—made from leaves and its own fur—Oranguru skillfully gives instructions to other Pokémon.
column6: It normally spends its time meditating in the treetops. It throws Poké Balls and gives other Pokémon orders as it pleases.
column7: Deep in the jungle, high in the lofty canopy, this Pokémon abides. On rare occasions, it shows up at the beach to match wits with Slowking.
column8: This Pokémon lives quietly in the depths of the forest. The purple, cape-like fur gets longer and longer as Oranguru ages.
column9: Known for its extreme intelligence, this Pokémon will look down on inexperienced Trainers, so it's best suited to veteran Trainers.
column10: It knows the forest inside and out. If it comes across a wounded Pokémon, Oranguru will gather medicinal herbs to treat it.

column3: Berries are its weapons as well as the staple of its diet. The one that can throw a berry the farthest is the boss of the troop.
column4: They battle with hard berries for weapons. Their techniques are passed from the boss to the group, generation upon generation.

column6: The boss chooses 10 members of the group to go out hunting. The hunting party will evenly split the food they find with the rest of the group.
column7: Displaying amazing teamwork, they follow the orders of their boss as they all help out in the search for their favorite berries.
column8: They form groups of roughly 20 individuals. Their mutual bond is remarkable—they will never let down a comrade.

column10: This Pokémon battles by throwing hard berries. It won’t obey a Trainer who throws Poké Balls without skill.

column3: This Pokémon is a coward. As it desperately dashes off, the flailing of its many legs leaves a sparkling clean path in its wake.
column4: Its habitat includes everything from beaches to seabeds. It is happy to eat up even rotten scraps, providing a sort of natural cleaning service.

column6: It will pick up anything it finds on the ground. Sometimes it finds coins, so Murkrow and Meowth will go after it.
column7: Wimpod gather in swarms, constantly on the lookout for danger. They scatter the moment they detect an enemy's presence.
column8: It's nature's cleaner—it eats anything and everything, including garbage and rotten things. The ground near its nest is always clean.
column9: Its habitat varies from beaches to seabeds. A natural scavenger, it will gleefully chow down on anything edible, no matter how rotten.

column3: With a flashing slash of its giant sharp claws, it cleaves seawater—or even air—right in two.
column4: It has the skill to cleave even air or seawater in two with a flash of its giant, sharp claws.
column5: It will do anything to win, taking advantage of every opening and finishing opponents off with the small claws on its front legs.
column6: It battles skillfully with its six arms, but spends most of its time peacefully meditating in caves deep beneath the sea.
column7: The shell covering its body is as hard as diamond. This Pokémon will do anything it takes to win.
column8: Its claws, which it can extend and retract at will, are its greatest weapons. Golisopod is sometimes accompanied by Wimpod.
column9: They live in sunken ships or in holes in the seabed. When Golisopod and Grapploct battle, the loser becomes the winner's meal.

column3: It uses four of its arms to fiercely assail its foes. Once they've been pushed to the brink of defeat, it finishes them off with the arms it kept hidden.
It takes control of anyone who puts a hand in its mouth. And so it adds to the accumulation of its sand-mound body.
column4: It likes the shovel on its head, so Sandygast will get serious and fight any children who come to take it back.
column5: Born from a sand mound playfully built by a child, this Pokémon embodies the grudges of the departed.
column6: If it loses its shovel, it will stick something else— like a branch—in its head to make do until it finds another shovel.
column7: If you build sand mounds when you're playing, destroy them before you go home, or they may get possessed and become Sandygast.
column8: Grudges of the dead have possessed a mound of sand and become a Pokémon. Sandygast is fond of the shovel on its head.
column9: Sandygast mainly inhabits beaches. It takes control of anyone who puts their hand into its mouth, forcing them to make its body bigger.
column10: It flings sand into the eyes of its prey and tries to close in on them while they're blinded, but since Sandygast is so slow, the prey tends to escape.

column3: Once it has whipped up a sandstorm to halt its opponents in their tracks, this terrifying Pokémon snatches away their vitality.
column4: The terrifying Palossand drags smaller Pokémon into its sandy body. Once its victims are trapped, it drains them of their vitality whenever it pleases.
column5: From the hollows in its arms, it fires the bones of its victims, which are all dried up after being drained of their vitality.
column6: Buried beneath the castle are masses of dried-up bones from those whose vitality it has drained.
column7: Palossand is known as the Beach Nightmare. It pulls its prey down into the sand by controlling the sand itself, and then it sucks out their souls.
column8: Each of its grains of sand has its own will. Palossand eats small Pokémon and siphons away their vital essence while they're still alive.
column9: Heaps of bones lie buried beneath its castle. They came from those whom Palossand drained of vitality and left all dried up.
column10: Possessed people controlled by this Pokémon transformed its sand mound into a castle. As it evolved, its power to curse grew ever stronger.
column11: This Pokémon lives on beaches, but it hates water. Palossand can't maintain its castle-like shape if it gets drenched by a heavy rain.

column3: It lives in warm, shallow waters. If it encounters a foe, it will spit out its internal organs as a means to punch them.
column4: The tradition known as Pyukumuku chucking started from the custom of throwing Pyukumuku back into the sea after they wash onshore.
column5: It's covered in a slime that keeps its skin moist, allowing it to stay on land for days without drying up.
column6: It lives in shallow seas, such as areas near a beach. It can eject its internal organs, which it uses to engulf its prey or battle enemies.

column3: A Pokémon weapon developed for a specific mission, it went berserk during an experiment, so it was cryogenically frozen.
column4: There's danger of its going on a rampage, so its true power is sealed away beneath its control mask.
column5: Rumor has it that the theft of top-secret research notes led to a new instance of this Pokémon being created in the Galar region.
column6: The heavy control mask it wears suppresses its intrinsic capabilities. This Pokémon has some hidden special power.
column7: It was modeled after a mighty Pokémon of myth. The mask placed upon it limits its power in order to keep it under control.
column8: Due to the danger that this synthetic Pokémon may go on a rampage, it wears a control mask to restrain its power.

column3: Although its name was Type: Null at first, the boy who evolved it into this form gave it the name by which it is now known.
column4: Through the bond it formed with its Trainer, its will was strengthened, and it was able to destroy its control mask.
column5: The final factor needed to release this Pokémon's true power was a strong bond with a Trainer it trusts.
column6: A solid bond of trust between this Pokémon and its Trainer awakened the strength hidden within Silvally. It can change its type at will.
column7: Its trust in its partner is what awakens it. This Pokémon is capable of changing its type, a flexibility that is well displayed in battle.
column8: This is its form once it has awakened and evolved. Freed from its heavy mask, its speed is greatly increased.

column3: This Pokémon is born in the ozone layer. It falls to the ground once the outer shell enclosing its core grows heavy.
column4: Originally making its home in the ozone layer, it hurtles to the ground when the shell enclosing its body grows too heavy.
column5: Although its outer shell is uncommonly durable, the shock of falling to the ground smashes the shell to smithereens.
column6: Strong impacts can knock it out of its shell. This Pokémon was born from mutated nanoparticles.
column7: It will react when touched or called to, but it’s unknown whether such responses indicate consciousness.
column8: It lives in the ozone layer, where it becomes food for stronger Pokémon. When it tries to run away, it falls to the ground.

column3: If its core stays exposed, it will soon die off. It's possible that it may survive if it's put into a Poké Ball quickly enough.
column4: It feeds on dust in the atmosphere. The color of its core is said to be determined by the composition of the dust it eats.
column5: It eats dust in the atmosphere. The composition of the dust determines the color of its core.
column6: Minior that fall to the ground try to return to space, but their wish will not come true—all they can do is fade away.
column7: This is its form when its shell has fallen off. The color of its core depends on the materials that made up the food it ate.
column8: Places where Minior fall from the night sky are few and far between, with Alola being one of the precious few.

column3: It stays asleep from the moment it's born. When it falls into a deep sleep, it stops moving altogether.
column4: It remains asleep from birth to death as a result of the sedative properties of the leaves that form its diet.
column5: Komala spends its entire life sleeping. It feeds on leaves that contain a potent poison only Komala can break down.
column6: A potent anesthetic can be made by diluting Komala's drool. This anesthetic was used for surgeries in the past.
column7: The log it holds was given to it by its parents at birth. It has also been known to cling to the arm of a friendly Trainer.
column8: It is born asleep, and it dies asleep. All its movements are apparently no more than the results of it tossing and turning in its dreams.

column3: Eating sulfur in its volcanic habitat is what causes explosive compounds to develop in its shell. Its droppings are also dangerously explosive.
column4: Explosive substances coat the shell on its back. Enemies that dare attack it will be blown away by an immense detonation.
column5: It lives in volcanoes and eats sulfur and other minerals. Materials from the food it eats form the basis of its explosive shell.
column6: Its exploding shell poses a real danger but is sensitive to moisture. On rainy days, Turtonator takes silent refuge in caves.
column7: It gushes fire and poisonous gases from its nostrils. Its dung is an explosive substance and can be put to various uses.
column8: The shell on its back is chemically unstable and explodes violently if struck. The hole in its stomach is its weak point.

column3: The long hairs on its back act as lightning rods. The bolts of lightning it attracts are stored as energy in its electric sac.
column4: The spiny fur on its back is normally at rest. When this Pokémon becomes agitated, its fur stands on end and stabs into its attackers.
column5: With the long hairs on its back, this Pokémon takes in electricity from other electric Pokémon. It stores what it absorbs in an electric sac.
column6: When it's in trouble, it curls up into a ball, makes its fur spikes stand on end, and then discharges electricity indiscriminately.
column7: When it's surprised or agitated, the 14 fur spikes on its back will stand up involuntarily.

column3: This Pokémon lives in dark places untouched by sunlight. When it appears before humans, it hides itself under a cloth that resembles a Pikachu.
column4: A lonely Pokémon, it conceals its terrifying appearance beneath an old rag so it can get closer to people and other Pokémon.

column6: A gust of wind revealed what hides under this Pokémon's rag to a passing Trainer, who went home and died painfully that very night.
column7: Its actual appearance is unknown. A scholar who saw what was under its rag was overwhelmed by terror and died from the shock.
column8: Mimikyu was only recently identified as a Pokémon. Previously, people thought it was just a ghost wearing a cloth.
column9: There was a scientist who peeked under Mimikyu's old rag in the name of research. The scientist died of a mysterious disease.
column10: Although it's a quiet, lonely Pokémon, if you try to look at what's under its rag, it will become agitated and resist violently.
column11: Sad that its true identity may be exposed, Mimikyu will mercilessly seek revenge on any opponent that breaks its neck.
column12: Its disguise made from an old rag allowed it to avoid an attack, but the impact broke the neck of the disguise. Now everyone knows it's a Mimikyu.
column13: Even as it struggles beneath the cloth to repair the broken neck of its disguise, Mimikyu keeps up its impersonation of Pikachu.
column14: If its neck is broken or its rag torn during an attack, it works through the night to patch it.
column15: After going to all the effort of disguising itself, its neck was broken. Whatever is inside is probably unharmed, but it's still feeling sad.
column16: There will be no forgiveness for any who reveal that it was pretending to be Pikachu. It will bring the culprit down, even at the cost of its own life.
column17: It has taken damage and can no longer hold the head of its cloth disguise upright. For as long as it lives, Mimikyu will never forget its attacker.
column18: Mimikyu is sad that it may be exposed for what it really is. It will take revenge on whoever broke the neck of its disguise-no forgiveness.
column19: It stands in front of a mirror, trying to fix its broken neck as if its life depended on it. It has a hard time getting it right, so it's crying inside.

column3: It burrows beneath the sand, radiating psychic power from the protuberance on its head. It waits for prey as it surveys the area.
column4: When it unleashes its psychic power from the protuberance on its head, the grating sound of grinding teeth echoes through the area.
column5: It stuns its prey with psychokinesis and then grinds them to mush with its strong teeth. Even Shellder's shell is no match for it.
column6: Its skin is thick enough to fend off Mareanie's spikes. With its robust teeth, Bruxish crunches up the spikes and eats them.
column7: When sunlight reflects on the ripples created by a Bruxish grinding its teeth, the water all around sparkles brilliantly.
column8: It grinds its teeth with great force to stimulate its brain. It fires the psychic energy created by this process from the protuberance on its head.

column3: It appears in towns and plays with the children. Drampa will protect kids when they're in danger, so their moms don't have to worry.
column4: If a child it has made friends with is bullied, Drampa will find the bully's house and burn it to the ground.
column5: It has a compassionate personality, but if it is angered, it completely destroys its surroundings with its intense breath.
column6: Drampa is a kind and friendly Pokémon—up until it's angered. When that happens, it stirs up a gale and flattens everything around.
column7: The mountains it calls home are nearly two miles in height. On rare occasions, it descends to play with the children living in the towns below.
column8: This Pokémon is friendly to people and loves children most of all. It comes from deep in the mountains to play with children it likes in town.

column3: Drampa's cells have been invigorated, allowing it to regain its youth. It manipulates the atmosphere to summon storms.
After lowering its anchor, it waits for its prey. It catches large Wailord and drains their life-force.
column4: After a piece of seaweed merged with debris from a sunken ship, it was reborn as this ghost Pokémon.
column5: It wraps its prey in green seaweed and sucks away their vitality. It only likes to go after big prey like Wailord.
column6: Its chain-like green seaweed can stretch outward for hundreds of yards. For some reason, it gets along well with Skrelp.
column7: Swinging its massive anchor, it can KO Wailord in a single blow. What appears to be green seaweed is actually its body.
column8: The soul of seaweed adrift in the waves became reborn as this Pokémon. It maintains itself with new infusions of seabed detritus and seaweed.

column3: They live in mountains where no trace of humans can be detected. Jangmo-o grow little by little as they battle one another.
column4: It will never show an enemy its back in battle. Ancient warriors liked Jangmo-o for its valiant disposition and made it one of their own.
column5: It expresses its feelings by smacking its scales. Metallic sounds echo through the tall mountains where Jangmo-o lives.
column6: It smacks the scales on its head against rocks or against the ground to frighten its opponents. It can also contact its friends with these noises.
column7: The scale atop its head is useful for both offense and defense. It bravely stands and faces its foes rather than turning tail, even if its foes are strong.
column8: Jangmo-o strikes its scales to communicate with others of its kind. Its scales are actually fur that's become as hard as metal.
column9: They communicate with their allies using the sounds their scales make when struck. A group of these Pokémon causes quite a racket.
column10: They learn to fight by smashing their head scales together. The dueling strengthens both their skills and their spirits.

column3: It gets into fistfights so intense that the scales on its arms peel off. Bare arms are a sign of valiance.
column4: It fires itself up by striking its scales with force as it dances. Its roar is a battle cry.
column5: The scales it sheds can be molded and processed to make pots and pans. They conduct heat well, so they're a popular item.
column6: It leaps at its prey with a courageous shout. Its scaly punches tear its opponents to shreds.
column7: Before attacking its enemies, it clashes its scales together and roars. Its sharp claws shred the opposition.
column8: It sheds and regrows its scales on a continuous basis. The scales become harder and sharper each time they're regrown.
column9: It makes noise by clanging its scales together. When the rhythm has reached its peak, Hakamo-o attacks.
column10: The scaleless, scarred parts of its body are signs of its strength. It shows them off to defeated opponents.

column3: It lets its arm hang, and then "quick as a flash" swings upward to land its specialty punch and send the opponent flying high into the sky.
column4: Certain ruins have paintings of ancient warriors wearing armor made of Kommo-o scales.
column5: It clatters its tail scales to unnerve opponents. This Pokémon will battle only those who stand steadfast in the face of this display.
column6: It battles valiantly with its fists, which are armored in steel scales. Kommo-o specializes in uppercuts.
column7: When it spots enemies, it threatens them by jingling the scales on its tail. Weak opponents will crack and flee in panic.
column8: It bashes its scales to test its opponents' mettle. The sound of struck Kommo-o scales frightens weaker foes and sends them running.
column9: Its rigid scales function as offense and defense. In the past, its scales were processed and used to make weapons and other commodities.
column10: When it howls after finishing off its prey, the metallic sounds of its celebrating comrades can be heard from all around.

column3: It confuses its enemies by flying too quickly for the eye to follow. It has a hair-trigger temper but forgets what made it angry an instant later.
column4: This guardian deity of Melemele is brimming with curiosity. It summons thunderclouds and stores their lightning inside its body.
column5: Although it's called a guardian deity, if a person or Pokémon puts it in a bad mood, it will become a malevolent deity and attack.
column6: The lightning-wielding guardian deity of Melemele, Tapu Koko is brimming with curiosity and appears before people from time to time.

column3: It heals the wounds of people and Pokémon by sprinkling them with its sparkling scales. This guardian deity is worshiped on Akala.
column4: This guardian deity of Akala is guilelessly cruel. The fragrant aroma of flowers is the source of its energy.
column5: As it flutters about, it scatters its strangely glowing scales. Touching them is said to restore good health on the spot.
column6: Although called a guardian deity, Tapu Lele is devoid of guilt about its cruel disposition and can be described as nature incarnate.

column3: Although it's called a guardian deity, it's violent enough to crush anyone it sees as an enemy.
column4: It pulls large trees up by the roots and swings them around. It causes vegetation to grow, and then it absorbs energy from the growth.
column5: The guardian deity of Ula'ula is a lazy Pokémon. It commands plants to immobilize its foes and then deals them a savage blow with its horns.
column6: It makes ringing sounds with its tail to let others know where it is, avoiding unneeded conflicts. This guardian deity of Ula'ula controls plants.

column3: Although it's called a guardian deity, terrible calamities sometimes befall those who recklessly approach Tapu Fini.
column4: The guardian deity of Poni, it can control water. People say it can create pure water that will wash away any uncleanness.
column5: This guardian deity of Poni Island manipulates water. Because it lives deep within a thick fog, it came to be both feared and revered.
column6: The dense fog it creates brings the downfall and destruction of its confused enemies. Ocean currents are the source of its energy.

column3: Whether or not it's a Pokémon from this world is a mystery. When it's in a jam, it warps away to a safe place to hide.
column4: Its body is gaseous and frail. It slowly grows as it collects dust from the atmosphere.
column5: In ages past, it was called the child of the stars. It's said to be a Pokémon from another world, but no specific details are known.
column6: Even though its helpless, gaseous body can be blown away by the slightest breeze, it doesn't seem to care.

column3: The king who ruled Alola in times of antiquity called it the “cocoon of the stars” and built an altar to worship it.
column4: Motionless as if dead, its body is faintly warm to the touch. In the distant past, it was called the cocoon of the stars.
column5: There's something accumulating around the black core within its hard shell. People think this Pokémon may come from another world.
column6: As it absorbs light, Cosmoem continues to grow. Its golden shell is surprisingly solid.

column3: Sometimes the result of its opening an Ultra Wormhole is that energy and life-forms from other worlds are called here to this world.
column4: It is said to live in another world. The intense light it radiates from the surface of its body can make the darkest of nights light up like midday.
column5: This Pokémon is said to be a male evolution of Cosmog. At the activation of its third eye, it departs for another world.

column3: It sometimes summons unknown powers and life-forms here to this world from holes that lead to other worlds.
column4: Said to live in another world, this Pokémon devours light, drawing the moonless dark veil of night over the brightness of day.
column5: It is said to be a female evolution of Cosmog. When its third eye activates, away it flies to another world.

column3: One of the Ultra Beasts. It's unclear whether or not this Pokémon is sentient, but sometimes it can be observed behaving like a young girl.
column4: One of several mysterious Ultra Beasts. People on the street report observing those infested by it suddenly becoming violent.
column5: A life-form from another world, it was dubbed a UB and is thought to produce a strong neurotoxin.
column6: It appeared in this world from an Ultra Wormhole. Nihilego appears to be a parasite that lives by feeding on people and Pokémon.

column3: Although it's alien to this world and a danger here, it's apparently a common organism in the world where it normally lives.
column4: This Ultra Beast appeared from another world. It shows off its body, but whether that display is a boast or a threat remains unclear.
column5: A mysterious life-form called an Ultra Beast. Witnesses saw it pulverize a dump truck with a single punch.
column6: Buzzwole goes around showing off its abnormally swollen muscles. It is one kind of Ultra Beast.

column3: One of the dangerous Ultra Beasts, it has been spotted running across the land at terrific speeds.
column4: A life-form that lives in another world, its body is thin and supple, but it also possesses great power.
column5: Although it's alien to this world and a danger here, it's apparently a common organism in the world where it normally lives.
column6: One of the Ultra Beasts. It refuses to touch anything, perhaps because it senses some uncleanness in this world.

column3: It appeared from the Ultra Wormhole. It raided a power plant, so people think it energizes itself with electricity.
column4: Although it's alien to this world and a danger here, it's apparently a common organism in the world where it normally lives.
column5: One of the mysterious life-forms known as Ultra Beasts. Astonishing electric shocks emanate from its entire body, according to witnesses.
column6: They've been dubbed Ultra Beasts. Some of them stand unmoving, like trees, with their arms and legs stuck into the ground.

column3: Although it's alien to this world and a danger here, it's apparently a common organism in the world where it normally lives.
column4: It appeared from the Ultra Wormhole. Witnesses observed it flying across the sky at high speed.
column5: One of the dangerous UBs, high energy readings can be detected coming from both of its huge arms.
column6: One kind of Ultra Beast. Witnesses have seen it burn down a forest by expelling gas from its two arms.

column3: This Ultra Beast came from the Ultra Wormhole. It seems not to attack enemies on its own, but its sharp body is a dangerous weapon in itself.
column4: Although it's alien to this world and a danger here, it's apparently a common organism in the world where it normally lives.
column5: One of the Ultra Beast life-forms, it was observed cutting down a gigantic steel tower with one stroke of its blade.
column6: This Ultra Beast's body, which is as thin as paper, is like a sharpened sword.

column3: It has gobbled mountains and swallowed whole buildings, according to reports. It's one of the Ultra Beasts.
column4: An unknown life-form called a UB. It may be constantly hungry—it is certainly always devouring something.
column5: A dangerous Ultra Beast, it appears to be eating constantly, but for some reason its droppings have never been found.
column6: Although it's alien to this world and a danger here, it's apparently a common organism in the world where it normally lives.

column3: It looks somehow pained as it rages around in search of light, which serves as its energy. It's apparently from another world.
column4: Light is apparently the source of its energy. It has an extraordinarily vicious disposition and is constantly firing off laser beams.
column5: Light is the source of its energy. If it isn't devouring light, impurities build up in it and on it, and Necrozma darkens and stops moving.
column6: Reminiscent of the Ultra Beasts, this life-form, apparently asleep underground, is thought to have come from another world in ancient times.

column3: This is Necrozma's form while it's absorbing the power of Solgaleo, making it extremely ferocious and impossible to control.
column4: This is its form while it is devouring the light of Solgaleo. It pounces on foes and then slashes them with the claws on its four limbs and back.

column3: This is its form while it's devouring the light of Lunala. It grasps foes in its giant claws and rips them apart with brute force.
column4: Lunala no longer has a will of its own. Now under the control of Necrozma, it continuously expels all of its energy.

column3: This is its form when it has absorbed overwhelming light energy. It fires laser beams from all over its body.
column4: The light pouring out from all over its body affects living things and nature, impacting them in various ways.

column3: It synchronizes its consciousness with others to understand their feelings. This faculty makes it useful for taking care of people.
column4: This artificial Pokémon, constructed more than 500 years ago, can understand human speech but cannot itself speak.
column5: Its mechanized body is merely a vessel. Its true self is its Soul-Heart, an artificial soul.
column6: This Pokémon was built by a scientist roughly 500 years ago. The part called the Soul-Heart is the actual life-form.
column7: Built roughly 500 years ago by a scientist, the part called the Soul-Heart is the actual life-form.
column8: This Magearna looks as it did at the time of its invention. Its magnificent ornamentation delighted the king's daughter.
column9: This is its form from almost 500 years ago. Its body is nothing more than a container—its artificial heart is the actual life-form.
column10: This gorgeous Magearna looks as it did at the time of its invention. Its luxurious decoration pleased the king's daughter.
column11: This beautiful form reflects Magearna's appearance when first presented to a king's daughter 500 years ago.
column12: It functions thanks to its artificial soul. The vivid decoration is proof that it was presented as a gift to royalty.

column3: This is Magearna once a previously hidden mode activates. The emotions Magearna had begun to feel now hide away as it fells foe after foe.
column4: A mechanism to remove Magearna's limitations has lain secretly within Magearna for 500 years. This mechanism is triggered by a Mega Stone.

column3: It slips into the shadows of others and mimics their powers and movements. As it improves, it becomes stronger than those it's imitating.
column4: It sinks into the shadows of people and Pokémon, where it can understand their feelings and copy their capabilities.
column5: Able to conceal itself in shadows, it never appears before humans, so its very existence was the stuff of myth.
column6: It slips into others' shadows and mimics their movements and powers. With time, its prowess will surpass that of those it originally imitated.
column7: It lurks in the shadows of others, copying their movements and powers. This Pokémon is craven and cowering.

column3: This Ultra Beast is well enough liked to be chosen as a first partner in its own world.
column4: An Ultra Beast that lives in a different world, it cackles wildly as it sprays its opponents with poison from the needles on its head.

column3: One kind of Ultra Beast, it fires a glowing, venomous liquid from its needles. This liquid is also immensely adhesive.
column4: It stores hundreds of liters of poisonous liquid inside its body. It is one of the organisms known as UBs.

column3: When stone walls started moving and attacking, the brute's true identity was this mysterious life-form, which brings to mind an Ultra Beast.
column4: It appeared from an Ultra Wormhole. Each one appears to be made up of many life-forms stacked one on top of each other.

column3: It slithers toward people. Then, without warning, it triggers the explosion of its own head. It's apparently one kind of Ultra Beast.
column4: A UB that appeared from an Ultra Wormhole, it causes explosions, then takes advantage of opponents' surprise to rob them of their vitality.

column3: Electricity sparks from the pads on its limbs. Wherever Zeraora runs, lightning flashes and thunder echoes.
column4: It electrifies its claws and tears its opponents apart with them. Even if they dodge its attack, they'll be electrocuted by the flying sparks.
column5: It approaches its enemies at the speed of lightning, then tears them limb from limb with its sharp claws.

column3: It stores up 10 lightning strikes' worth of electricity. When it stops limiting itself, it's in the strongest class of electric Pokémon.
It melts particles of iron and other metals found in the subsoil, so it can absorb them into its body of molten steel.
column4: Its body is made up of molten steel. It melts particles of iron and other metals it finds in the ground and absorbs them into itself.

column3: It was worshipped long ago for its capacity to produce iron. For some reason, it has come back to life after 3,000 years.
column4: Revered long ago for its capacity to create iron from nothing, for some reason it has come back to life after 3,000 years.

column3: Centrifugal force is behind the punches of Melmetal's heavy hex-nut arms. Melmetal is said to deliver the strongest punches of all Pokémon.
column4: At the end of its life-span, Melmetal will rust and fall apart. The small shards left behind will eventually be reborn as Meltan.
column5: It can send electric beams streaking out from the hole in its belly. The beams' tremendous energy can vaporize an opponent in one shot.
column6: In a distant land, there are legends about a cyclopean giant. In fact, the giant was a Melmetal that was flooded with Gigantamax energy

column3: The stick Grookey holds has grown harder and more flexible after soaking in the energy that emanates from inside Grookey’s body.
column4: When it uses its special stick to strike up a beat, the sound waves produced carry revitalizing energy to the plants and flowers in the area.
column5: As Grookey strikes everything in sight with its stick, it has more and more fun, and its rhythm becomes livelier.
column6: It attacks with rapid beats of its stick. As it strikes with amazing speed, it gets more and more pumped.

column3: The faster a Thwackey can beat out a rhythm with its two sticks, the more respect it wins from its peers.
column4: When it's drumming out rapid beats in battle, it gets so caught up in the rhythm that it won't even notice that it's already knocked out its opponent.
column5: It becomes intensely focused while it drums out vigorous beats—so much so that sometimes it won’t listen to its Trainer’s commands.
column6: It skillfully uses its two sticks not only for drumming out rapid beats but also for pummeling opponents during battle.

column3: Though it has a calm disposition, it won’t tolerate those who drum up discord. It strictly disciplines offenders until they learn their lesson.
column4: By drumming, it taps into the power of its special tree stump. The roots of the stump follow its direction in battle.
column5: The one with the best drumming techniques becomes the boss of the troop. It has a gentle disposition and values harmony among its group.
column6: When one Rillaboom challenges another who leads a troop, a drumming battle commences. The victor becomes the troop’s new boss.

column3: Gigantamax energy has caused Rillaboom's stump to grow into a drum set that resembles a forest.
column4: Rillaboom has become one with its forest of drums and continues to lay down beats that shake all of Galar.

column3: A warm-up of running around gets fire energy coursing through this Pokémon's body. Once that happens, it's ready to fight at full power.
column4: Once its body has heated up, Scorbunny can use the full extent of its power. That’s why it does warm-up exercises.
column5: It has special pads on the backs of its feet, and one on its nose. Once it's raring to fight, these pads radiate tremendous heat.
column6: Fire energy gathers in the pads of its feet, raising their temperature. Once hot, Scorbunny’s footpads can deal heavy damage to opponents.

column3: Its thick and fluffy fur protects it from the cold and enables it to use hotter fire moves.
column4: While it prides itself on its varied kicking moves, it can also deliver powerful headbutts once its flames have heated up its forehead.
column5: Thanks to its soft and fluffy fur, Raboot can easily heat up its fire energy and produce flames with more power.
column6: It kicks berries right off the branches of trees and then juggles them with its feet, practicing its footwork.

column3: It juggles a pebble with its feet, turning it into a burning soccer ball. Its shots strike opponents hard and leave them scorched.
column4: Using a ball that it made out of flames, Cinderace blinds its opponents and keeps them at bay.
column5: It's skilled at both offense and defense, and it gets pumped up when cheered on. But if it starts showboating, it could put itself in a tough spot.
column6: Although Cinderace loses its cool easily, it will battle flawlessly for a Trainer it trusts.

column3: Gigantamax energy can sometimes cause the diameter of this Pokémon's fireball to exceed 300 feet.
column4: Infused with Cinderace's fighting spirit, the gigantic Pyro Ball never misses its targets and completely roasts opponents.

column3: It’s a very cautious Pokémon. When it has no choice but to battle, it hides itself before attacking.
column4: When it gets wet, its skin changes color, and this Pokémon becomes invisible as if it were camouflaged.
column5: It gets berries to eat by shooting them down with bullets of water it spurts from its mouth. Its aim is perfect.
column6: When scared, this Pokémon cries. Its tears pack the chemical punch of 100 onions, and attackers won't be able to resist weeping.

column3: Highly intelligent but also very lazy, it defends its home by laying traps.
column4: Highly intelligent but also very lazy, it keeps enemies out of its territory by laying traps everywhere.
column5: A clever combatant, this Pokémon battles using water balloons created with moisture secreted from its palms.
column6: It extends its long, slimy tongue at blinding speeds and finishes off its prey with great skill.

column3: It has many hidden capabilities, such as fingertips that can shoot water and a membrane on its back that it can use to glide through the air.
column4: It may present itself as being well-mannered, but deep down, it still has a lazy side. It will slack off when its Trainer isn’t looking.
column5: It uses special lenses in its eyes to sense things about its opponents—like their body heat—then attacks once it identifies a weak spot.
column6: Its nictitating membranes let it pick out foes' weak points so it can precisely blast them with water that shoots from its fingertips at Mach 3.

column3: It has excellent sniping skills. Shooting a berry rolling along over nine miles away is a piece of cake for this Pokémon.
column4: Gigantamax Inteleon's Water Gun move fires at Mach 7. As the Pokémon takes aim, it uses the crest on its head to gauge wind and temperature.

column3: Found throughout the Galar region, this Pokémon becomes uneasy if its cheeks are ever completely empty of berries.
column4: It eats berries nonstop—a habit that has made it more resilient than it looks. It'll show up on farms, searching for yet more berries.
column5: It stores berries in its cheeks. When there are no berries to be found, Skwovet will stuff pebbles into its cheeks to stave off its cravings.
column6: No matter how much it stuffs its belly with food, it is always anxious about getting hungry again. So, it stashes berries in its cheeks and tail.

column3: Common throughout the Galar region, this Pokémon has strong teeth and can chew through the toughest of berry shells.
column4: It stashes berries in its tail—so many berries that they fall out constantly. But this Pokémon is a bit slow-witted, so it doesn't notice the loss.
column5: Growing up with a family of foodies, this Pokémon learned to love cooking. It will happily eat pretty much anything, so it tends to season food rather carelessly.
column6: This Pokémon makes off with heaps of fallen berries by wrapping them in its tail, which is roughly twice the length of its body.
column7: If it spots a berry tree, it will immediately go to gather berries without a sideways glance—even if it’s in the middle of a battle.

column3: The females are fussier than the males. If another creature dirties a female Rookidee's wings, it'll peck the offender relentlessly in a burning rage.
column4: This valiant Pokémon will challenge any foe, no matter how mighty. Even if that backfires and Rookidee loses, it grows from the experience.
column5: Jumping nimbly about, this small-bodied Pokémon takes advantage of even the slightest opportunity to disorient larger opponents.
column6: It will bravely challenge any opponent, no matter how powerful. This Pokémon benefits from every battle—even a defeat increases its strength a bit.
column7: This Pokémon is brave and reckless. The white markings around a Rookidee's eyes intimidate fainthearted Pokémon.

column3: Corvisquire are intelligent enough to use tools. They'll use their feet to pick up and fling pebbles or wrap their enemies in ropes.
column4: Smart enough to use tools in battle, these Pokémon have been seen picking up rocks and flinging them or using ropes to wrap up enemies.
column5: It’s said that the reason behind Corvisquire’s high level of intelligence is the large size of its brain relative to those of other bird Pokémon.
column6: The lessons of many harsh battles have taught it how to accurately judge an opponent's strength.
column7: This intelligent Pokémon will quickly learn how to use any tool it can hold in its beak or its talons.

column3: Its wings, now made of steel, are extremely tough. Even if it's attacked mid-flight, Corviknight flies on mightily through the sky, unbothered.
column4: With their great intellect and flying skills, these Pokémon very successfully act as the Galar region's airborne taxi service.
column5: Corviknight can’t serve as a taxi service in Paldea because the Pokémon’s natural predators will attack it while it flies, endangering the customer.
column6: This Pokémon reigns supreme in the skies of the Galar region. The black luster of its steel body could drive terror into the heart of any foe.
column7: Although its wings have partly turned to steel and become heavy as a result, this Pokémon flies through the skies with ease.

column3: Imbued with Gigantamax energy, its wings can whip up winds more forceful than any a hurricane could muster. The gusts blow everything away.
column4: The eight feathers on its back are called blade birds, and they can launch off its body to attack foes independently.

column3: A constant collector of information, this Pokémon is very smart. Very strong is what it isn't.
column4: Often found in gardens, this Pokémon has hairs on its body that it uses to assess its surroundings.

column3: As it grows inside its shell, it uses its psychic abilities to monitor the outside world and prepare for evolution.
column4: It barely moves, but it's still alive. Hiding in its shell without food or water seems to have awakened its psychic powers.

column3: It's famous for its high level of intelligence, and the large size of its brain is proof that it also possesses immense psychic power.
column4: It emits psychic energy to observe and study what's around it—and what's around it can include things over six miles away.

column3: If it were to utilize every last bit of its power, it could control the minds of every living being in its vicinity.
column4: Its brain has grown to a gargantuan size, as has the rest of its body. This Pokémon's intellect and psychic abilities are overpowering.

column3: Cunning and cautious, this Pokémon survives by stealing food from others. It erases its tracks with swipes of its tail as it makes off with its plunder.
column4: It filches food from people and Pokémon alike. While fleeing the scene, it wipes its tracks away with its tail to throw off pursuers.
column5: Aided by the soft pads on its feet, it silently raids the food stores of other Pokémon. It survives off its ill-gotten gains.

column3: It secretly marks potential targets with a scent. By following the scent, it stalks its targets and steals from them when they least expect it.
column4: This clever Pokémon marks potential targets with its scent, then follows its nose to locate their nests.
column5: With a lithe body and sharp claws, it goes around stealing food and eggs. Boltund is its natural enemy.

column3: It anchors itself in the ground with its single leg, then basks in the sun. After absorbing enough sunlight, its petals spread as it blooms brilliantly.
column4: It whirls around in the wind while singing a joyous song. This delightful display has charmed many into raising this Pokémon.

column3: The seeds attached to its cotton fluff are full of nutrients. It spreads them on the wind so that plants and other Pokémon can benefit from them.
column4: The cotton on the head of this Pokémon can be spun into a glossy, gorgeous yarn—a Galar regional specialty.

column3: Its curly fleece is such an effective cushion that this Pokémon could fall off a cliff and stand right back up at the bottom, unharmed.
column4: If its fleece grows too long, Wooloo won't be able to move. Cloth made with the wool of this Pokémon is surprisingly strong.

column3: Its majestic horns are meant only to impress the opposite gender. They never see use in battle.
column4: Weave a carpet from its springy wool, and you end up with something closer to a trampoline. You'll start to bounce the moment you set foot on it.

column3: It starts off battles by attacking with its rock-hard horn, but as soon as the opponent flinches, this Pokémon bites down and never lets go.
column4: A popular game among children is to lift up sticks that Chewtle have bitten onto to see whose Chewtle can hang on the longest.
column5: Its large front tooth is still growing in. When the tooth itches, this Pokémon will bite another Chewtle’s horn, and the two Pokémon will tussle.
column6: Apparently the itch of its teething impels it to snap its jaws at anything in front of it.

column3: Drednaw lurks along the shoreline. When prey come to drink water, Drednaw stretches its neck out and chomps down on them.
column4: Its massive, jagged teeth can crush a boulder in a single bite. This Pokémon has an extremely vicious disposition.
column5: This Pokémon rapidly extends its retractable neck to sink its sharp fangs into distant enemies and take them down.
column6: With jaws that can shear through steel rods, this highly aggressive Pokémon chomps down on its unfortunate prey.

column3: It responded to Gigantamax energy by becoming bipedal. First it comes crashing down on foes, and then it finishes them off with its massive jaws.
column4: In the Galar region, there's a tale about this Pokémon chewing up a mountain and using the rubble to stop a flood.

column3: This Pokémon is very popular as a herding dog in the Galar region. As it runs, it generates electricity from the base of its tail.
column4: This gluttonous Pokémon only assists people with their work because it wants treats. As it runs, it crackles with electricity.

column3: It sends electricity through its legs to boost their strength. Running at top speed, it easily breaks 50 mph.
column4: This Pokémon generates electricity and channels it into its legs to keep them going strong. Boltund can run nonstop for three full days.

column3: Chunks of the surface of this Pokémon's body that have grown old and flaked off have long been used for fuel as an alternative to coal.
column4: Most of its body has the same composition as coal. Fittingly, this Pokémon was first discovered in coal mines about 400 years ago.
column5: On sunny days, Rolycoly will come out onto grassy plains and roll around. Then it will eat the grass that gets tangled in its wheel-like leg.
column6: It can race around like a unicycle, even on rough, rocky terrain. Burning coal sustains it.

column3: The temperature inside its body increases when it experiences strong emotions. It rolls around frantically while spewing flames.
column4: By rapidly rolling its legs, it can travel at over 18 mph. The temperature of the flames it breathes exceeds 1,800 degrees Fahrenheit.
column5: Due to the coal tar created inside it, the heap of coal on Carkol's back never falls apart, even when the Pokémon rolls around at high speeds.
column6: It forms coal inside its body. Coal dropped by this Pokémon once helped fuel the lives of people in the Galar region.

column3: It's usually peaceful, but the vandalism of mines enrages it. Offenders will be incinerated with flames that reach 2,700 degrees Fahrenheit.
