column4: As a result of Rose's meddling, Eternatus absorbed all the energy in the Galar region. It's now in a state of power overload.

column3: Kubfu trains hard to perfect its moves. The moves it masters will determine which form it takes when it evolves.
column4: If Kubfu pulls the long white hair on its head, its fighting spirit heightens and power wells up from the depths of its belly.

column3: This form of Urshifu is a strong believer in the one-hit KO. Its strategy is to leap in close to foes and land a devastating blow with a hardened fist.
column4: Inhabiting the mountains of a distant region, this Pokémon races across sheer cliffs, training its legs and refining its moves.

column3: It's believed that this Pokémon modeled its fighting style on the flow of a river—sometimes rapid, sometimes calm.
column4: This form of Urshifu is a strong believer in defeating foes by raining many blows down on them. Its strikes are nonstop, flowing like a river.

column3: People call it the embodiment of rage. It's said that this Pokémon's terrifying expression and shout will rid the world of malevolence.
column4: The energy released by this Pokémon's fists forms shock waves that can blow away Dynamax Pokémon in just one hit.

column3: All it takes is a glare from this Pokémon to take the lives of those with evil in their hearts—or so they say.
column4: As it waits for the right moment to unleash its Gigantamax power, this Pokémon maintains a perfect one-legged stance. It won't even twitch.

column3: Within dense forests, this Pokémon lives in a pack with others of its kind. It's incredibly aggressive, and the other Pokémon of the forest fear it.
column4: Once the vines on Zarude's body tear off, they become nutrients in the soil. This helps the plants of the forest grow.

column3: This Pokémon is a cluster of electrical energy. It's said that removing the rings on Regieleki's body will unleash the Pokémon's latent power.
column4: Its entire body is made up of a single organ that generates electrical energy. Regieleki is capable of creating all Galar's electricity.

column3: An academic theory proposes that Regidrago's arms were once the head of an ancient dragon Pokémon. The theory remains unproven.
column4: Its body is composed of crystallized dragon energy. Regidrago is said to have the powers of every dragon Pokémon.

column3: Glastrier has tremendous physical strength, and the mask of ice covering its face is 100 times harder than diamond.
column4: Glastrier emits intense cold from its hooves. It's also a belligerent Pokémon—anything it wants, it takes by force.

column3: It probes its surroundings with all its senses save one—it doesn't use its sense of sight. Spectrier's kicks are said to separate soul from body.
column4: As it dashes through the night, Spectrier absorbs the life-force of sleeping creatures. It craves silence and solitude.

column3: Calyrex is known in legend as a king that ruled over Galar in ancient times. It has the power to cause hearts to mend and plants to spring forth.
column4: Calyrex is a merciful Pokémon, capable of providing healing and blessings. It reigned over the Galar region in times of yore.

column3: It's said that this Pokémon once moved a large forest—and all the Pokémon living there—to a new location overnight.
column4: According to lore, this Pokémon showed no mercy to those who got in its way, yet it would heal its opponents' wounds after battle.

column3: Legend says that by using its power to see all events from past to future, this Pokémon saved the creatures of a forest from a meteorite strike.
column4: It's said that Calyrex and a Pokémon that had bonded with it ran all across the Galar region to bring green to the wastelands.

column3: The black orbs shine with an uncanny light when the Pokémon is erecting invisible barriers. The fur shed from its beard retains heat well and is a highly useful material for winter clothing.
This Pokémon is a rough, crude, and violent sort. It swings around its large, heavy stone axes to finish off its prey.
column4: Parts of its body turned to stone when it evolved thanks to an extremely rare ore found in volcanic areas.
column5: Its stone axes get chipped away here and there during ferocious battles, but the process serves to make them sharper and sharper.
column6: A violent creature that fells towering trees with its crude axes and shields itself with hard stone. If one should chance upon this Pokémon in the wilds, one's only recourse is to flee.

column3: It crossed the sea and drifted ashore in a new land. Surviving in this place led it to take on a unique appearance and gain special powers.
column4: This special Ursaluna can see in the dark with its left eye and protects itself with mud that is as hard as iron.
column5: I believe it was Hisui's swampy terrain that gave Ursaluna its burly physique and newfound capacity to manipulate peat at will.

column3: This special Ursaluna can see in the dark with its left eye and protects itself with mud that is as hard as iron.
column4: It crossed the sea and drifted ashore in a new land. Surviving in this place led it to take on a unique appearance and gain special powers.

column3: It can jump with incredible power. Parts of its body are tinged red by the rage of its fallen friends.
column4: This Pokémon is cloaked in the souls of its comrades who perished during a punishing journey to the river of their birth.
column5: Clads itself in the souls of comrades that perished before fulfilling their goals of journeying upstream. No other species throughout all Hisui's rivers is Basculegion's equal.

column3: It can afflict a target with terrifying illusions that are under its control. The deeper the sadness in its friends' souls, the paler Basculegion becomes.
column4: The souls of its comrades cloak its body and propel it effortlessly through even raging rapids.

column3: Because of Sneasler's virulent poison and daunting physical prowess, no other species could hope to best it on the frozen highlands. Preferring solitude, this species does not form packs.
Overqwil's poisonous spikes-now thicker, longer, and stronger-will automatically strike toward anything that moves.
column4: It’s ferocious and has a short temper. The ends of its spikes are barbed, and they can’t be easily removed once they’ve punctured something.
column5: Its lancelike spikes and savage temperament have earned it the nickname "sea fiend." It slurps up poison to nourish itself.
column6: Overqwil’s poisonous spikes will reflexively react even while Overqwil sleeps, stabbing any moving thing that approaches.

column3: When it flies to this land from across the sea, the bitter winter comes to an end. According to legend, this Pokémon's love gives rise to the budding of fresh life across Hisui.
A different guise from its feminine humanoid form. From the clouds, it descends upon those who treat any form of life with disrespect and metes out wrathful, ruthless punishment.
The sweet scent its body gives off mesmerizes those around it. The scent grows stronger when this Pokémon is in the sun.
column4: Its fluffy fur is similar in composition to plants. This Pokémon frequently washes its face to keep it from drying out.

column3: Floragato deftly wields the vine hidden beneath its long fur, slamming the hard flower bud against its opponents.
column4: The hardness of Floragato's fur depends on the Pokémon's mood. When Floragato is prepared to battle, its fur becomes pointed and needle sharp.

column3: This Pokémon uses the reflective fur lining in its cape to camouflage the stem of its flower, creating the illusion that the flower is floating.
column4: With skillful misdirection, it rigs foes with pollen-packed flower bombs. Meowscarada sets off the bombs before its foes realize what's going on.

column3: It lies on warm rocks and uses the heat absorbed by its square-shaped scales to create fire energy.
column4: Its flame sac is small, so energy is always leaking out. This energy is released from the dent atop Fuecoco's head and flickers to and fro.

column3: The valve in Crocalor's flame sac is closely connected to its vocal cords. This Pokémon utters a guttural cry as it spews flames every which way.
column4: The combination of Crocalor’s fire energy and overflowing vitality has caused an egg-shaped fireball to appear on the Pokémon’s head.

column3: Skeledirge’s gentle singing soothes the souls of all that hear it. It burns its enemies to a crisp with flames of over 5,400 degrees Fahrenheit.
column4: The fiery bird changes shape when Skeledirge sings. Rumor has it that the bird was born when the fireball on Skeledirge’s head gained a soul.

column3: This Pokémon migrated to Paldea from distant lands long ago. The gel secreted by its feathers repels water and grime.
column4: Its strong legs let it easily swim around in even fast-flowing rivers. It likes to keep things tidy and is prone to overthinking things.

column3: These Pokémon constantly run through shallow waters to train their legs, then compete with each other to see which of them kicks most gracefully.
column4: The hardworking Quaxwell observes people and Pokémon from various regions and incorporates their movements into its own dance routines.

column3: Dancing in ways that evoke far-away places, this Pokémon mesmerizes all that see it. Flourishes of its decorative water feathers slice into its foes.
column4: A single kick from a Quaquaval can send a truck rolling. This Pokémon uses its powerful legs to perform striking dances from far-off lands.

column3: It searches for food all day. It possesses a keen sense of smell but doesn’t use it for anything other than foraging.
column4: This Pokémon spurns all but the finest of foods. Its body gives off an herblike scent that bug Pokémon detest.

column3: Oinkologne is proud of its fine, glossy skin. It emits a concentrated scent from the tip of its tail.
column4: It entrances female Pokémon with the sweet, alluring scent that wafts from all over its body.

column3: This Pokémon sends a flowerlike scent wafting about. Well-developed muscles in its legs allow it to leap more than 16 feet with no trouble at all.
column4: This is a meticulous Pokémon that likes to keep things tidy. It shrouds itself in a floral aroma that soothes the Pokémon around it.

column3: The ball of threads wrapped around its body is elastic enough to deflect the scythes of Scyther, this Pokémon’s natural enemy.
column4: The thread it secretes from its rear is as strong as wire. The secret behind the thread's strength is the topic of ongoing research.

column3: It clings to branches and ceilings using its threads and moves without a sound. It takes out its prey before the prey even notices it.
column4: Spidops covers its territory in tough, sticky threads to set up traps for intruders.

column3: It's highly skilled at a fighting style in which it uses its jumping capabilities to dodge incoming attacks while also dealing damage to opponents.
column4: It has its third set of legs folded up. When it’s in a tough spot, this Pokémon jumps over 30 feet using the strength of its legs.

column3: It uses its normally folded third set of legs when in Showdown Mode. This places a huge burden on its body, so it can't stay in this mode for long.
column4: When it decides to fight all out, it stands on its previously folded legs to enter Showdown Mode. It neutralizes its enemies in short order.

column3: It has underdeveloped electric sacs on its cheeks. These sacs can produce electricity only if Pawmi rubs them furiously with the pads on its forepaws.
column4: The pads of its paws are electricity-discharging organs. Pawmi fires electricity from its forepaws while standing unsteadily on its hind legs.

column3: When its group is attacked, Pawmo is the first to leap into battle, defeating enemies with a fighting technique that utilizes electric shocks.
column4: Pawmo uses a unique fighting technique in which it uses its forepaws to strike foes and zap them with electricity from its paw pads simultaneously.

column3: This Pokémon normally is slow to react, but once it enters battle, it will strike down its enemies with lightning-fast movements.
column4: Pawmot's fluffy fur acts as a battery. It can store the same amount of electricity as an electric car.

column3: Exhibiting great teamwork, they use their incisors to cut pieces out of any material that might be useful for a nest, then make off with them.
column4: The pair sticks together no matter what. They split any food they find exactly in half and then eat it together.

column3: The two little ones just appeared one day. The group might be a family of related Pokémon, but nobody knows for sure.
column4: The larger pair protects the little ones during battles. When facing strong opponents, the whole group will join the fight.
column5: They build huge nests with many rooms that are used for different purposes, such as eating and sleeping.
column6: The little one just appeared one day. They all live together like a family, but the relationship between the three is still unclear.

column3: This Pokémon cultivates yeast within its body. It has lived with humans—and helped enrich their diets—since ancient times.
column4: The yeast in Fidough's breath is useful for cooking, so this Pokémon has been protected by people since long ago.
column5: This Pokémon is smooth and moist to the touch. Yeast in Fidough’s breath induces fermentation in the Pokémon’s vicinity.

column3: The appetizing scent of Dachsbun's body sends opponents' hunger into overdrive, wearing down their willpower and stamina.
column4: The pleasant aroma that emanates from this Pokémon’s body helps wheat grow, so Dachsbun has been treasured by farming villages.
column5: The surface of this Pokémon's skin hardens when exposed to intense heat, and its body has an appetizing aroma.

column3: It protects itself from enemies by emitting oil from the fruit on its head. This oil is bitter and astringent enough to make someone flinch.
column4: This Pokémon converts nutrients into oil, which it stores in the fruit on its head. It can easily go a whole week without eating or drinking.

column3: Dolliv shares its tasty, fresh-scented oil with others. This species has coexisted with humans since times long gone.
column4: It basks in the sun to its heart's content until the fruits on its head ripen. After that, Dolliv departs from human settlements and goes on a journey.

column3: This Pokémon drives back enemies by launching its rich, aromatic oil at them with enough force to smash a boulder.
column4: This calm Pokémon is very compassionate. It will share its delicious, nutrient-rich oil with weakened Pokémon.

column3: Green-feathered flocks hold the most sway. When they're out searching for food in the mornings and evenings, it gets very noisy.
column4: Squawkabilly form flocks based on the color of their plumage. Green ones are abundant because their coloration is more likely to be passed down.
column5: These Pokémon prefer to live in cities. They form flocks based on the color of their feathers, and they fight over territory.

column3: It was born in a layer of rock salt deep under the earth. This species was particularly treasured in the old days, as they would share precious salt.
column4: The ground scrapes its body as it travels, causing it to leave salt behind. Salt is constantly being created and replenished inside Nacli's body.
column5: As Nacli treads along the ground, grains of rock salt are left behind. These trails have been called "salt roads" since long ago.

column3: This absent-minded Pokémon tends to forget about the prey it has salt-cured. It's even prone to forgetting the orders its Trainer has given it.
column4: This Pokémon dry cures its prey by spraying salt over them. The curing process steals away the water in the prey’s body.
column5: It compresses rock salt inside its body and shoots out hardened salt pellets with enough force to perforate an iron sheet.

column3: Many Pokémon gather around Garganacl, hoping to lick at its mineral-rich salt.
column4: Garganacl will rub its fingertips together and sprinkle injured Pokémon with salt. Even severe wounds will promptly heal afterward.
column5: Rock salt obtained from Garganacl has many restorative elements and is helpful for maintaining one's health.

column3: Burnt charcoal came to life and became a Pokémon. Possessing a fiery fighting spirit, Charcadet will battle even tough opponents.
column4: This Pokémon is skilled at manipulating flames. Humans have prized it highly since ancient times.
column5: Its firepower increases when it fights, reaching over 1,800 degrees Fahrenheit. It likes Berries that are rich in fat.

column3: Though Armarouge pledges loyalty to its Trainer, it repeatedly leaves to go train on its own to strengthen its psychic energy.
column4: This Pokémon clads itself in armor that has been fortified by psychic and fire energy, and it shoots blazing fireballs.
column5: Armarouge evolved through the use of a set of armor that belonged to a distinguished warrior. This Pokémon is incredibly loyal.

column3: The fiery blades on its arms burn fiercely with the lingering resentment of a sword wielder who fell before accomplishing their goal.
column4: An old set of armor steeped in grudges caused this Pokémon's evolution. Ceruledge cuts its enemies to pieces without mercy.
column5: It swears loyalty to its Trainer. Should anyone dare to show that Trainer hostility, Ceruledge will cut the offender down without a second thought.

column3: It floats using the electricity stored in its body. When thunderclouds are around, Tadbulb will float higher off the ground.
column4: Tadbulb shakes its tail to generate electricity. If it senses danger, it will make its head blink on and off to alert its allies.

column3: What appear to be eyeballs are actually organs for discharging the electricity generated by Bellibolt's belly-button dynamo.
column4: When this Pokémon expands and contracts its wobbly body, the belly-button dynamo in its stomach produces a huge amount of electricity.

column3: When its wings catch the wind, the bones within produce electricity. This Pokémon dives into the ocean, catching prey by electrocuting them.
column4: These Pokémon make their nests on coastal cliffs. The nests have a strange, crackling texture, and they're a popular delicacy.

column3: Kilowattrel inflates its throat sac to amplify its electricity. By riding the wind, this Pokémon can fly over 430 miles in a day.
column4: It uses its throat sac to store electricity generated by its wings. There's hardly any oil in its feathers, so it is a poor swimmer.

column3: Packs of these Pokémon are incredibly close-knit. In order to be taken seriously, Maschiff intimidate others by wearing a grumpy expression.
column4: Its well-developed jaw and fangs are strong enough to crunch through boulders, and its thick fat makes for an excellent defense.
column5: It always scowls in an attempt to make opponents take it seriously, but even crying children will burst into laughter when they see Maschiff’s face.

column3: Mabosstiff loves playing with children. Though usually gentle, it takes on an intimidating look when protecting its family.
column4: This Pokémon can store energy in its large dewlap. Mabosstiff unleashes this energy all at once to blow away enemies.
column5: Contrary to appearances, Mabosstiff has a kind heart-but it will relentlessly chase down and put an end to any foe that dares threaten its family.

column3: Shroodle protects itself from foes using both its poisonous saliva and its front teeth, which are disproportionately big and long for its small body.
column4: Though usually a mellow Pokémon, it will sink its sharp, poison-soaked front teeth into any that anger it, causing paralysis in the object of its ire.
column5: To keep enemies away from its territory, it paints markings around its nest using a poisonous liquid that has an acrid odor.

column3: Each Grafaiai paints its own individual pattern, and it will paint that same pattern over and over again throughout its life.
column4: The color of the poisonous saliva depends on what the Pokémon eats. Grafaiai covers its fingers in its saliva and draws patterns on trees in forests.
column5: What Grafaiai eats affects the color of its saliva. With this saliva, it paints patterns on rocks and trees in order to protect its territory.

column3: Not even Bramblin knows where it is headed as it tumbles across the wilderness, blown by the wind. It loathes getting wet.
column4: A soul unable to move on to the afterlife was blown around by the wind until it got tangled up with dried grass and became a Pokémon.

column3: Brambleghast wanders around arid regions. On rare occasions, mass outbreaks of these Pokémon will bury an entire town.
column4: It will open the branches of its head to envelop its prey. Once it absorbs all the life energy it needs, it expels the prey and discards it.

column3: Though it looks like Tentacool, Toedscool is a completely different species. Its legs may be thin, but it can run at a speed of 30 mph.
column4: Toedscool lives in muggy forests. The flaps that fall from its body are chewy and very delicious.

column3: It coils its 10 tentacles around prey and sucks out their nutrients, causing the prey pain. The folds along the rim of its head are a popular delicacy.
column4: These Pokémon gather into groups and form colonies deep within forests. They absolutely hate it when strangers approach.

column3: Klawf hangs upside-down from cliffs, waiting for prey. But Klawf can’t remain in this position for long because its blood rushes to its head.
column4: This Pokémon lives on sheer cliffs. It sidesteps opponents' attacks, then lunges for their weak spots with its claws.

column3: Traditional Paldean dishes can be extremely spicy because they include the shed front teeth of Capsakid among their ingredients.
column4: Capsakid is prone to intense mood swings, perhaps influenced by the spicy chemicals inside it. It will bite anything it takes a disliking to.
column5: The more sunlight this Pokémon bathes in, the more spicy chemicals are produced by its body, and thus the spicier its moves become.

column3: Its two heads have different temperaments and excel at different fighting styles. Nevertheless, they act in perfect harmony on the battlefield.
column4: The green head has turned vicious due to the spicy chemicals stimulating its brain. Once it goes on a rampage, there is no stopping it.
column5: The red head converts spicy chemicals into fire energy and blasts the surrounding area with a super spicy stream of flame.

column3: Mega Evolution has dialed up this Pokémon's spiciness. It swings its "necktie" around to wallop its foes.
It rolls its mud ball around while the energy it needs for evolution matures. Eventually the time comes for it to evolve.
column4: This Pokémon creates a mud ball by mixing sand and dirt with psychic energy. It treasures its mud ball more than its own life.

column3: The body that supports the ball barely moves. Therefore, it is thought that the true body of this Pokémon is actually inside the ball.
column4: An infant sleeps inside the ball. Rabsca rolls the ball soothingly with its legs to ensure the infant sleeps comfortably.

column3: It spends its time running around wastelands. If anyone steals its beloved berries, it will chase them down and exact its revenge.
column4: Flittle’s toes levitate about half an inch above the ground because of the psychic power emitted from the frills on the Pokémon’s belly.

column3: It emits psychic power from the gaps between its multicolored frills and sprints at speeds greater than 120 mph.
column4: It immobilizes opponents by bathing them in psychic power from its large eyes. Despite its appearance, it has a vicious temperament.

column3: It swings its handmade hammer around to protect itself, but the hammer is often stolen by Pokémon that eat metal.
column4: This Pokémon pounds iron scraps together to make a hammer. It will remake the hammer again and again until it's satisfied with the result.
column5: Tinkatink cobbles together a suitable hammer for itself using tools and metal fragments it finds in mines.

column3: These Pokémon make their homes in piles of scrap metal. They test the strength of each other's hammers by smashing them together.
column4: This Pokémon will attack groups of Pawniard and Bisharp, gathering metal from them in order to create a large and sturdy hammer.
column5: Tinkatuff sometimes come to human settlements, where they rummage through factories and warehouses to gather metal.

column3: The hammer tops 220 pounds, yet it gets swung around easily by Tinkaton as it steals whatever it pleases and carries its plunder back home.
column4: A Tinkaton leader teaches the Tinkatink in its group the skills needed for pounding and shaping metal.
column5: A Tinkaton that is particularly skilled with its hands, allowing it to make good use of other tools in addition to its hammer. It is very curious about buildings and machines made by humans.
column6: This intelligent Pokémon has a very daring disposition. It knocks rocks into the sky with its hammer, aiming for flying Corviknight.

column3: This Pokémon can pick up the scent of a Veluza just over 65 feet away and will hide itself in the sand.
column4: Though it looks like Diglett, Wiglett is an entirely different species. The resemblance seems to be a coincidental result of environmental adaptation.

column3: It has a vicious temperament, contrary to what its appearance may suggest. It wraps its long bodies around prey, then drags the prey into its den.
column4: A variety of fish Pokémon, Wugtrio was once considered to be a regional form of Dugtrio.

column3: It gathers things up in an apron made from shed feathers added to the Pokémon’s chest feathers, then drops those things from high places for fun.
column4: Bombirdier uses the apron on its chest to bundle up food, which it carries back to its nest. It enjoys dropping things that make loud noises.

column3: Its water ring is made from seawater mixed with a sticky fluid that Finizen secretes from its blowhole.
column4: It likes playing with others of its kind using the water ring on its tail. It uses ultrasonic waves to sense the emotions of other living creatures.

column3: This Pokémon changes its appearance if it hears its allies calling for help. Palafin will never show anybody its moment of transformation.
column4: Its physical capabilities are no different than a Finizen's, but when its allies are in danger, it transforms and powers itself up.

column3: This Pokémon's ancient genes have awakened. It is now so extraordinarily strong that it can easily lift a cruise ship with one fin.
column4: This hero of the ocean swims at a speed of 50 knots and saves drowning people and Pokémon.

column3: The steel section is Varoom's actual body. This Pokémon clings to rocks and converts the minerals within into energy to fuel its activities.
column4: It is said that this Pokémon was born when an unknown poison Pokémon entered and inspirited an engine left at a scrap-processing factory.

column3: It creates a gas out of poison and minerals from rocks. It then detonates the gas in its cylinders—now numbering eight—to generate energy.
column4: Revavroom viciously threatens others with the sound of its exhaust. It sticks its tongue out from its cylindrical mouth and sprays toxic fluids.

column3: It can sprint at over 70 mph while carrying a human. The rider's body heat warms Cyclizar's back and lifts the Pokémon's spirit.
column4: Cyclizar keeps energy stored in the folds of its throat, allowing it to run nonstop over very long distances while carrying someone.
column5: Apparently Cyclizar has been allowing people to ride on its back since ancient times. Depictions of this have been found in 10,000-year-old murals.

column3: When attacked, this Pokémon will wield the tendrils on its body like fists and pelt the opponent with a storm of punches.
column4: This Pokémon lives in arid deserts. It maintains its metal body by consuming iron from the soil.

column3: Glimmet clings to the ceilings of caves. It defends itself by striking enemies with its bud, which is packed with poisonous components.
column4: It absorbs nutrients from cave walls. The petals it wears are made of crystallized poison.
column5: Glimmet's toxic mineral crystals look just like flower petals. This Pokémon scatters poisonous powder like pollen to protect itself.

column3: Glimmora blooms into a large flower made of poisonous components. It works together with groups of Glimmet to repel enemies.
column4: Glimmora's petals are made of crystallized poison energy. It has recently become evident that these petals resemble Tera Jewels.
column5: When this Pokémon detects danger, it will open up its crystalline petals and fire beams from its conical body.

column3: Glimmora's petals—now larger and separated from its main body—rotate around it to provide defense while scattering poisonous fragments.
It is said that a dog Pokémon that died in the wild without ever interacting with a human was reborn as this Pokémon.
column4: This friendly Pokémon doesn't like being alone. Pay it even the slightest bit of attention, and it will follow you forever.
column5: This Pokémon loves to gambol and roughhouse. It rubs its fur up against others and absorbs their life force until they're utterly drained.

column3: A lovingly mourned Pokémon was reborn as Houndstone. It doesn't like anyone touching the protuberance atop its head.
column4: Houndstone spends most of its time sleeping in graveyards. Among all the dog Pokémon, this one is most loyal to its master.
column5: At some point during the many years it spent guarding the gravestone of its dearly departed master, it was reborn as a ghost Pokémon.

column3: This Pokémon apparently ties the base of its neck into a knot so that energy stored in its belly does not escape from its beak.
column4: A flock of Flamigo can reach numbers of over 10,000. The sight of one of these massive flocks moving together in sync is incredible to behold.
column5: Thanks to a behavior of theirs known as “synchronizing,” an entire flock of these Pokémon can attack simultaneously in perfect harmony.

column3: This species left the ocean and began living on land a very long time ago. It seems to be closely related to Wailmer.
column4: It lives in frigid regions in pods of five or so individuals. It loves the minerals found in snow and ice.

column3: Ice energy builds up in the horn on its upper jaw, causing the horn to reach cryogenic temperatures that freeze its surroundings.
column4: This Pokémon wanders around snowy, icy areas. It protects its body with powerful muscles and a thick layer of fat under its skin.

column3: When Veluza discards unnecessary flesh, its mind becomes honed and its psychic power increases. The spare flesh has a mild but delicious flavor.
column4: Veluza has excellent regenerative capabilities. It sheds spare flesh from its body to boost its agility, then charges at its prey.

column3: It treats Tatsugiri like its boss and follows it loyally. Though powerful, Dondozo is apparently not very smart.
column4: Although it boasts fantastic strength and a huge body that stirs up great waves when it thrashes, Dondozo struggles at catching prey.
column5: This Pokémon is a glutton, but it's bad at getting food. It teams up with a Tatsugiri to catch prey.

column3: Tatsugiri will battle furiously among themselves to determine which of them will get to partner with a particular Dondozo.
column4: Tatsugiri is an extremely cunning Pokémon. It feigns weakness to lure in prey, then orders its partner to attack.
column5: This is a small dragon Pokémon. It lives inside the mouth of Dondozo to protect itself from enemies on the outside.

column3: This Pokémon tricks its opponents by playing dead. It is small and weak, but it uses its smarts to survive.
column4: This species' differing colors and patterns are apparently the result of Tatsugiri changing itself to suit the preferences of the prey it lures in.
column5: Even if swallowed by a foe, Tatsugiri will try to make its escape through various means, such as by inflating its throat sac.

column3: Tatsugiri assesses situations using its sharp intellect. If it determines there is no hope of winning, it flees as fast as it can.
column4: It's one of the most intelligent dragon Pokémon. It camouflages itself by inflating its throat sac.
column5: Bird Pokémon are its main prey. This Pokémon knows it's weak, so it hunts with a partner.

column3: Tatsugiri's brain has been invigorated by Mega Evolution, making it even wilier. It can create and command copies of itself.
It solidifies the energy of Mega Evolution, building up an overflowing pile to launch as projectiles. These projectiles explode on contact.
Using the energy of Mega Evolution, it creates a dish to ride upon, allowing it to move with total freedom-even through the air.
When its anger rose beyond a critical point, this Pokémon gained power that is unfettered by the limits of its physical body.
column4: It imbues its fists with the power of the rage that it kept hidden in its heart. Opponents struck by these imbued fists will be shattered to their core.
column5: Primeape evolved into Annihilape after repeatedly using a move with enough force to burst the blood vessels in its head.

column3: When attacked, this Pokémon will retaliate by sticking thick spines out from its body. It’s a risky move that puts everything on the line.
column4: It lives at the bottom of ponds and swamps. It will carry Wooper on its back and ferry them across water from one shore to the other.

column3: The hardened head from the tail protects the head of the main body as Farigiraf whips its long neck around to headbutt enemies.
column4: Now that the brain waves from the head and tail are synced up, the psychic power of this Pokémon is 10 times stronger than Girafarig’s.
